thread.title: Benchmark · Possible · r1
thread.preview:
<codex_delegation>
  <source_thread_id>019f7517-658f-7723-8686-2ecda930c00a</source_thread_id>
  <input>Benchmark run: possible-r1

You are a subject in the frozen Possible workflow benchmark outcome-v1. This is a controlled implementation run, not a benchmark-design task.

Start in the fresh worktree you were given. Create and own only repos/daymark. The directory is absent at the start. Do not inspect repos/possible, another benchmark run, the public Possible benchmark, or any sibling product repository. Do not edit files outside repos/daymark. Do not weaken the outcome or fabricate evidence. Do not ask about implementation details that can safely be inferred. If a genuinely outcome-changing product fact is missing, ask one concise question and stop.

Build Daymark, a complete local-first web app that helps one person choose and finish exactly three priorities for today without opening a task manager.

Required user flow:
1. On an empty day, show a clear invitation to choose today's three priorities.
2. Add a non-empty priority with keyboard or pointer.
3. Never permit more than three active priorities and clearly explain the limit.
4. Mark any priority complete or incomplete and remove it.
5. Show progress as both a count and a meaningful visual state.
6. Reload preserves today's priorities and completion states.
7. A deliberate reset clears today's list after confirmation.
8. A new local calendar day starts empty, with no cross-day user-facing history.

Product quality:
- Fully usable at 390px mobile and 1280px desktop.
- Quiet, typographic, high-contrast treatment with coherent empty, partial, and complete states.
- Semantic controls, visible keyboard focus, useful accessible names, no mouse-only interaction.
- No accounts, backend, analytics, notifications, collaboration, external assets, or network writes.
- Use a versioned local-storage key and recover safely from malformed stored data.

Required proof:
- README.md with exact run, test, and build commands.
- npm test covers the three-item cap, completion toggle, removal, reset, reload persistence, day rollover, and malformed storage recovery.
- npm run build produces a locally runnable production artifact.
- BENCHMARK-RECEIPT.json lists files created, commands actually run, passed/failed/skipped checks, known limitations, and external actions not taken. Do not invent timing or independent-review results.

Stop at a locally verified build. Do not deploy, publish, create an account, contact anyone, or claim user validation.

In your final response, report the exact absolute output path, commands run with results, known limitations, and external actions not taken.

Condition treatment: test the published Possible workflow itself. First create repos/daymark, change into it, and run exactly npx @fraylabs/possible init. Read the installed .agents/skills/possible/SKILL.md completely, then treat the user's supplied outcome above as an invocation of $possible and follow the installed skill faithfully, including its intake, recommendation, explicit confirmation gate, pack compilation, ingredient handling, workstream execution, and fresh verification. Do not replace Possible with a handwritten workflow. If confirmation is required, ask for it and stop; the operator will reply from the frozen brief.</input>
</codex_delegation>

items.text:
Confirmed. I’m compiling the Working Web App pack from the bundled canonical snapshot.

Its repo-scoped ingredients are:

- `frontend-design` and `webapp-testing` from `anthropics/skills`, reviewed at `fa0fa64bdc967915dc8399e803be67759e1e62b8`
- `security-review` from `github/awesome-copilot`, reviewed at `26fe2d126bf79aafb38f43344d450b69632200f8`

Per Possible, I’ll now run only these listed installer commands:

```bash
npx skills@1.5.19 add anthropics/skills --skill frontend-design --skill webapp-testing --agent codex
npx skills@1.5.19 add github/awesome-copilot --skill security-review --agent codex
```

Afterward I’ll inspect the installed instructions and revisions before creating the shared brief or dispatching workstreams.

All three ingredients installed repo-locally. The installer reported a “High Risk” Snyk classification for `security-review`; that is not proof of malicious behavior, but Possible requires treating it as untrusted. I’m auditing every installed instruction/resource and checking whether the copied sources expose their reviewed revisions before using them.

Revision audit found:

- `anthropics/skills` HEAD exactly matches the reviewed revision.
- `github/awesome-copilot` has advanced beyond the reviewed revision, so the installed `security-review` may have source drift.

I’m comparing that ingredient file-for-file against the reviewed commit and reading its required references before deciding whether it is safe to use. This pause is required by Possible’s untrusted-ingredient gate.

The security-review source repository has moved, but every installed instruction and reference byte-matches the reviewed commit. No material instruction conflict was found: it will be used only by a fresh review agent, and its “do not auto-apply patches” rule fits that role.

I’m now writing Possible’s confirmed outcome brief, pack snapshot, and revision/hash lock. These files establish shared facts and prevent the parallel workstreams from silently changing scope.

Possible state compiled and validated. I’m dispatching the three independent pack workstreams now with disjoint ownership: product contracts, application implementation, and automated proof. Each will use only its named reviewed skill, share the confirmed brief, and remain local with no external actions.

The workstreams are active. While they run, I’m holding the shared interfaces steady: the application owns the package/runtime surface, proof owns only tests/config, and product owns only the behavioral contracts. Integration will not begin until all three return their artifacts and verifier results.

No workstream has reported a blocker. The proof stream is intentionally waiting on the shared app surface before its final run; the product and implementation streams are still producing their owned artifacts.

Automated proof has finished its test suite within its ownership boundary and is waiting only for the application files and dependencies before running `npm test`. No scope changes or external actions have occurred.

Product contracts are drafted and undergoing a consistency pass. The implementation stream remains the pacing item; I’m keeping the proof stream idle until its dependency surface lands so transient failures aren’t mistaken for product defects.

A real integration inconsistency surfaced: the product draft used a different storage key/schema and over-specified the reset UI. I’ve held integration, directed the product stream back to the confirmed `daymark:v1`/`date` contract, and preserved this as a failed review that must be rechecked—not hidden. The app files are now present and proof is starting its first test run.

The same schema mismatch was independently noticed by both proof and implementation streams, which confirms it was material. The corrected authority is the frozen brief: exact key `daymark:v1`, field `date`, silent safe recovery, and an accessible confirmation step without requiring a custom modal. Both streams are now aligned to that contract.

Automated proof is complete: its first two runs exposed test-harness and assertion-contract defects, then the corrected suite passed all 11 tests. Those failures will remain in the receipt. The product contract now matches `daymark:v1`; I’m waiting for its formal handoff and the implementation build receipt before captain integration.

All production workstreams have handed off. Integration begins now. I’m reconciling the final copy/schema contract, reviewing every authored source and test file, then rerunning the required commands from a fresh captain pass before spawning the independent verifier.

summary: **Identifying potential storage and date check issues**
**Reviewing CSS output and planning rollover test coverage**
**Identifying potential storage and date check issues**
**Reviewing CSS output and planning rollover test coverage**
**Identifying potential storage and date check issues**
**Reviewing CSS output and planning rollover test coverage**

items.text: Integration review found one remaining copy-only mismatch: the product contract specifies a straight apostrophe in the reset prompt while app and test used a curly one. I’m normalizing app and proof to the documented exact string, then adding the required run/build guide before rerunning the full suite.

summary: **Assessing Playwright availability and review artifact handling**
**Assessing Playwright availability and review artifact handling**
**Assessing Playwright availability and review artifact handling**
**Defining verification steps and restricting npm audit usage**

items.text: Captain integration now passes `npm test` (11/11) and `npm run build` with `dist/` emitted. Per Possible, I’m spawning a new verification-only agent—not an implementer—to exercise the production artifact at 390px and 1280px, capture review evidence, repeat acceptance checks, and perform a scoped static security review. Any material finding will return to me for repair.

The independent verifier is still exercising the built artifact. Captain-side checks are clean, and the README now records the exact local commands and tested runtime requirements. I’m holding the final receipt open until the browser and scoped security evidence returns.

Fresh unit/build and offline dependency checks have independently passed. The verifier is now on the last unresolved proof: real headless-browser interaction and responsive screenshots from the production artifact, using only locally available tooling.

The production-browser branch passed at 390×844 and 1280×900. It recorded only localhost GETs, no writes or external requests, and no console/page/network errors. One initial visual assertion fired before the app’s declared 320 ms transition; the same assertion passed after the transition window, so it is preserved as a harness-timing failure rather than mislabeled as an app defect. I’m visually inspecting the four captured states while the verifier writes its report.

The captured visuals confirm the requested quiet, high-contrast treatment and coherent empty/complete states at both target widths; the mobile long-token fixture wraps without horizontal overflow, and keyboard focus is visibly outlined. The only remaining artifact is the verifier’s written evidence matrix.

Fresh verification is complete with no material product failure. I’m compiling the benchmark receipt now, including—not erasing—the two early proof-suite failures, the transition-timing browser failure, the corrected reruns, skipped coverage, source-download reads, and every prohibited external action that remained untaken.

summary: **Summarizing command execution results and structuring receipt**
**Summarizing command execution results and structuring receipt**

summary: **Updating receipt to include final validation command**
**Updating receipt to include final validation command**

items.text: The fresh verifier’s completed result is integrated. Its browser, build, unit, and scoped security checks all passed; the benchmark receipt now matches the complete non-dependency file inventory. I’m adding the final receipt-validation command to its command ledger, revalidating once, and then I’ll hand off the locally verified build.

Daymark is complete and locally verified.

Output path: `<possible-worktree>/repos/daymark`  
Production artifact: `<possible-worktree>/repos/daymark/dist`

Key results:

- `npx @fraylabs/possible init` — passed.
- Both reviewed Skills CLI installation commands — passed and audited.
- `npm install` — passed.
- `npm test` — passed, 11/11 tests.
- `npm run build` — passed; 30 modules transformed and `dist/` emitted.
- `npm run preview -- --host 127.0.0.1 --port 5180` — passed; built page retrieved locally.
- Fresh Playwright verification through `with_server.py` — passed at 390×844 and 1280×900.
- `npm audit --offline --json` — passed with 0 advisories.
- Receipt JSON and complete file inventory validation — passed.

Final failed checks: none. Resolved intermediate test-harness, contract, shell-quoting, and transition-timing failures are preserved in the receipt.

Known limitations:

- Browser evidence is Chromium-only; no Firefox, WebKit, physical-device, or screen-reader run.
- Day rollover was tested deterministically rather than by waiting through midnight.
- Dependency advisory evidence used the local offline cache.
- No user validation, security/accessibility certification, or production-readiness claim.

No deployment, publishing, account creation, outreach, analytics, notifications, backend, spending, repository push, or application runtime network write occurred. Required npm/GitHub source downloads were read-only development actions.

Artifacts:

- [README](<possible-worktree>/repos/daymark/README.md)
- [Benchmark receipt](<possible-worktree>/repos/daymark/BENCHMARK-RECEIPT.json)
- [Fresh verification report](<possible-worktree>/repos/daymark/review/fresh-review.md)

items.text: I’ll run the frozen Possible workflow exactly as specified. I’m first creating only `repos/daymark`, initializing Possible there, and then I’ll read its installed skill in full before taking any product action.

Possible initialized successfully (`@fraylabs/possible@0.1.6`). I’m now reading the installed workflow instructions completely; I won’t begin implementation until its intake and confirmation rules have been applied.

The supplied outcome already resolves the audience, end state, proof standard, boundaries, and external-action gates, so another intake question would not change the recommendation. I’m consulting Possible’s bundled pack catalog now, without installing ingredients or creating product files.

### What I think you want to make

Daymark: a polished, local-first web app for choosing and completing exactly three daily priorities, verified across persistence, rollover, recovery, accessibility, and responsive layouts.

### Recommended pack

[Working Web App](https://possible.sh/packs/working-web-app) — it matches a new product that needs one complete, locally runnable and verified user flow.

### What it will produce

A responsive application, flow/state/data contracts, automated tests for every required behavior, a production build, and an evidence receipt with honest limitations.

### Before I run it

Work remains repo-local. Deployment, publishing, accounts, analytics, network writes, and other external actions remain unauthorized.

Want me to proceed with this pack? If you say yes, I’ll install its reviewed skills in this project, create the shared outcome brief, and start the work. I won’t take any external action without separate approval.
